data.title: Will Crypto ETFs Have Lasting Appeal?
data.description:
By Ivan Castano

Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) price may have fallen from last year's highs, but that's not stopping a slew of ETFs from courting investors in hopes that the king of crypto will rebound this year.

Regulatory tailwinds, such as the SEC's recent passage of generic listings standards (GLS) and the upcoming The Digital Asset Market Clarity (CLARITY) Act, are also expected to boost investor interest in these funds, which raised a whopping $47.2 billion last year, despite $5 billion of withdrawals in the fourth quarter.

Traditional finance (TradFi) remains bullish on digital currencies, and many institutions are set to enter the space in the coming months, potentially helping ETF inflows to more than double in 2026, analysts say.

"We will absolutely see more inflows this year," said Chris Matta, CEO of consultancy Cryptocollective.io, adding that he expects 25 to 50 new such funds to launch this year. "Though BTC went from roughly $120,000 to $90,000 last year, you still had a huge amount of inflows."

Institutions such as banks and asset managers have warmed up to digital currencies amid Washington's policy changes and GLS's rollout. The provisions have been a boon to the $3.3 trillion industry, sharply streamlining ETF listings and sparking a second wave of applications featuring popular altcoins such as Doge (DOGE-USD), Cardano (ADA-USD) and Polkadot (DOT-USD).

This momentum is also being reflected in the derivatives market. In February, CME Group expanded its crypto derivatives suite with the launch of Cardano, Chainlink and Stellar futures. This comes off the back of a year of records for the exchange's crypto product suite, with total notional volume up 75% in 2025 vs 2024.

Regulatory Shifts

Before GLS, crypto ETFs were treated as "special cases" requiring cumbersome filings subject to long delays. Under the new rules, if a crypto asset meets certain preset criteria, an exchange can list it within five days and without an SEC vote.

GLS requires a coin to be traded as a futures asset for at least six months, have a 12-month average liquidity of $700 million and be part of the fraud-preventing Intermarket Surveillance Group (ISG) to gain approval.

The regulation has encouraged a string of banks, such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs, to launch their own crypto ETFs. It has also given rise to so-called basket ETFs incorporating diverse coins, with the Nasdaq Crypto US Index (NCIUS) (which holds Bitcoin, Ethereum (ETH-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), XRP (XRP-USD), Cardano and more) being a key example. In January of this year, it was announced that the Nasdaq Crypto US Index would become the Nasdaq CME Crypto Index (NCI).

“The announcement brings together two of the world’s most trusted market infrastructure providers at a pivotal moment for the digital asset ecosystem, as renewed investor confidence and clearer regulatory frameworks accelerate institutional participation,” the Nasdaq Newsroom reported.

Institutional Adoption

Meanwhile, U.S. banks and asset managers have begun advising clients to hold crypto as part of a diversified portfolio.

The latest is Bank of America, which is allowing its network of 15,000 advisors to recommend spot Bitcoin ETFs. It is also advising clients to hold 1% to 4% of total assets in crypto, following on the heels of similar moves by Morgan Stanley, Fidelity, JP Morgan and Wells Fargo.

Morgan Stanley recently announced the launch of a Solana ETF with staking rewards. Called the Morgan Stanley Solana Trust, the vehicle pays 6.5% to 7.7% annually into the fund. ETF issuers are adding proof-of-stake (PoS) rewards that help run coin networks to boost their funds' appeal. These can be paid into the fund or through an annual investor dividend.

The SEC is currently reviewing applications for a handful of other vehicles, including those for Doge, Cardano, Polkadot and Avalanche (AVAX-USD). As one of the first major altcoins, Cardano is known for its ultra-secure network and reliable 'liquid staking.' Meanwhile, Polkadot and Avalanche distinguish themselves by offering faster transaction speeds, lower costs and higher staking rewards.

Clarity in Focus

Looking at future regulations, all eyes are currently set on the CLARITY Act, the administration's latest regulation that could benefit crypto. Currently in Congress, the bill is expected to win approval in the first half.

Within CLARITY, digital assets will be classified as "Digital Commodities" under the CFTC instead of as "securities" under the SEC's purview. This means fund issuers won't have to worry about being sued for holding 'unregistered securities.'

The provision also lowers the regulatory burden for banks to hold crypto ETFs as custodians, encouraging conservative pension funds and sovereign wealth funds to enter the space, experts say. CLARITY also calls for ETFs to be treated like stablecoins, enabling real time, 24/7 settlement.

"CLARITY is going to help bolster trading and custody for Bitcoin," said Bitcoin consultant Mark Connors. “As banks begin to act as custodians, they will get their trading, research and advisory arms behind the business, deepening adoption."

CLARITY is facing some speed bumps, however. The Senate rejected it on January 16 over a dispute concerning stablecoin yields, casting uncertainty on whether it will be legislated before the U.S.'s mid-term elections.

Crypto-focused lawyer Carol Van Cleef said the CLARITY Act's delay is unlikely to dent Bitcoin's long-term prospects. She added that other factors, such as volatility surrounding U.S. foreign policy, could play a bigger role.

"CLARITY will give Bitcoin a shot in the arm but there are so many factors in the environment that could affect its price," she said. "We have seen a lot of technological developments and use cases have exploded in the last few years, all without a major overhaul of the regulatory structures."

If CLARITY passes and other crypto catalysts, such as the U.S.'s growing twin deficits – simultaneous budget and trade deficits – and expectations of two more rate cuts this year, materialize, Bitcoin could reverse course.

Amid this potential momentum, Connors forecasts that ETF inflows could gain 1x to 1.5x this year. And in 2027, as more institutions and sovereign wealth funds move into the space, they could swell by 1.5x to 2x, he said.

Last year, sovereign funds, notably Qatar's QIA, Norway's NBIM and Abu Dhabi's ADIA/Mubdala, purchased BTC directly or via stakes in BTC proxies like MicroStrategy. Analysts expect the trend to continue over the next 12-24 months.

Bear Market?

But critics say not so fast. In a recent report, CryptoQuant said Bitcoin has entered a bear market, adding that spot Bitcoin funds cut their holdings by about 24,000 BTC in the fourth quarter of 2025 and that it doesn't expect those flows to recover fast.

Bitcoin fell below its 365-day moving average ($101,000) last November, a level the consultancy said has historically signaled a bear market. Even after bouncing roughly 21% by early January, it still remained technically under water, the consultancy added.

But Kat Liu, a portfolio manager at ETF manager Ipox Schuster, is unconcerned. "If it doesn't go lower than $60,000, I wouldn't worry," she said. "BTC is less speculative than five years ago. Institutional investors, pension funds and hedge funds still want to get in."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UniCredit: Premium Profitability Deserves A Premium Valuation
data.description:
Having delivered a surge in profits in recent years, how much could be retained in a lower interest rate environment has been a key question facing Italian bank UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY) (OTCPK:UNCFF). Well, management has delivered an emphatic answer, guiding for the bank's return on tangible equity to rise from 19% in 2025 to around 25% by 2030.

Unthinkable a few years ago, this durability has been well rewarded by the market. UniCredit's ADSs have returned around 24% since my last update last summer, a run that has left its shares at around 1.9 times tangible book value.

Recommending the stock at that kind of valuation isn't easy, but with UniCredit in a position to return substantial amounts of capital to shareholders in the years ahead, I remain bullish. I rate shares "Buy".

Resilient Net Interest Revenue

UniCredit revealed its 2025 results this week. Despite a backdrop of lower European interest rates, full-year net income grew 14% to €10.1 billion, while the return on tangible equity improved 150 bps to 19.2%.

The durability of UniCredit's net interest revenue has been a key part of my investment case for the bank. In theory, UniCredit should be sensitive to lower interest rates. That's because it benefits enormously from a large stock of low-cost domestic funds, with the bank paying just 0.27% on its Italian deposits last quarter. With limited ability to reduce this much further when interest rates fall, UniCredit should see flagging interest margins and revenue.

With that in mind, NIR has held up well. While it did fall 4.3% last year to €13.7 billion, this is off a cycle high. Quarterly trends suggest it may have already troughed, as NIR grew €60 million sequentially last quarter to €3.43 billion.

UniCredit's deposit hedging program has contributed to this performance. To keep things simple, readers can imagine this as being like a large bond portfolio, funded by the bank's rate-insensitive deposits (non-interest-bearing transaction accounts, for instance). The nominal size of this hedge was €203 billion last quarter, with management reporting an average maturity of 5 years. In other words, this hedge still contains investments that were taken out before interest rates rose. As these investments mature, the proceeds can be put to work at prevailing rates, which should drive the portfolio's yield higher.

This appears to be happening, with UniCredit reporting a 1.42% yield on its deposit hedge last quarter, up 17 bps from the time of my last piece. As this remains below current euro swap rates, repricing should continue to be a tailwind for the bank.

Good Work On Fees…

Non-interest revenue has been another crucial component of UniCredit's investment case, as it can obviously help alleviate any pressure from lower NIR.

In contrast to, say, the United States, many European markets have seen less disintermediation in financial services. So, in addition to gathering deposits and making loans, that means banks in these markets are often leaders in areas like asset management and insurance as well.

That being the case, I have been particularly interested in the moves UniCredit is making in asset management. The bank has over 14 million retail customers to target, so this is a lucrative area to explore.

After selling off its asset management arm in the late-2010s to raise capital, UniCredit has been steadily rebuilding its capabilities. For example, its 'onemarkets' fund range now has over €30 billion in assets under management, up over 100% from 2024. Some of these funds are managed in-house, others by external managers like JPMorgan (JPM) and BlackRock (BLK). Importantly, UniCredit estimated that it retains around 80% of the value from its funds offering, and it aims to grow this to over 85% by 2028.

UniCredit reported €2.61 billion in fees from investment products last year, a 9% rise on 2024. As a share of total income, this line increased almost 100 bps to 10.6%, meaning it is now UniCredit's largest source of fee income. Now, because managed assets typically won't sit on the bank's balance sheet, this income is inherently capital light. This should help support UniCredit's return on tangible equity, especially as management wants to double onemarkets AUM by 2028.

…And Costs

Given resilient NIR and good growth in fees, UniCredit's revenue has held up well in the face of lower interest rates. Cost control has been important in ensuring that this drops down to the bank's earnings, with operating expenses rising just 0.4% last year to €9.44 billion.

With a significant physical branch network, UniCredit has been able to extract efficiencies from this part of its cost base, offsetting headwinds elsewhere. For example, a net reduction in headcount has helped mitigate wage inflation, leading to just 0.3% growth in HR costs last year.

Meanwhile, credit costs remain relatively subdued. Loan loss provisions were €662 million last year, an increase of around €20 million from 2024. Relative to total loans, provisions were flat at 15 bps. Credit trends remain solid, with Stage 3 loans holding steady at around 2.7% of total lending.

Of course, UniCredit can only partially control its credit costs, as the quality of its loans will be highly sensitive to macroeconomic developments. That said, the bank does retain a circa €1.7 billion stock of macro overlays. These are general provisions that were raised to reflect risks that typical credit models may not capture (COVID and geopolitical tensions, for instance). In the past, I have described these as "use them or lose them" reserves. Either they are needed, in which case the bank has already booked the credit charge, or they aren't, in which case they will need to be released back through the income statement. This will help keep a lid on future credit costs, supporting the bank's earnings.

Worth A Premium

On the back of its strong operating performance, UniCredit's valuation has continued to increase. Shares now trade for €74.14 (around $43.97 per ADS), which is equal to 1.88 times last quarter's tangible book value. This is up from roughly 1.57 when I last wrote on the bank.

Paying that kind of price would have seemed out of the question a few years ago. These days, I am not sure it is. With management guiding for a 20%-plus return on tangible equity this year, UniCredit's price-to-earnings ratio is only around 10. This quickly falls into the single digits based on 2027-2030 guidance, with net income seen rising to at least €15 billion by the end of this period.

It is worth thinking about the capital returns that this will produce. With UniCredit earning such strong returns on its equity, it is having no trouble returning lots of cash to shareholders. Indeed, having distributed €36 billion over the 2021-2025 period, management expects to return €50 billion across 2026-2030. That's equal to around 45% of the company's current market cap. Plus, as UniCredit is sitting on a healthy 14.7% Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio, it even has scope to exceed this. With that pointing to a circa 8-9% annual shareholder yield, I think UniCredit is worthy of its premium valuation. Accordingly, I continue to rate shares "Buy".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: John Hancock 2035 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced solid returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outperformed its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

What you should know before investing

The portfolio's performance depends on the advisor's skill in determining asset class allocations, the mix of underlying funds, and the performance of those underlying funds. The portfolio is subject to the same risks as the underlying funds and exchange-traded funds in which it invests: Stocks and bonds can decline due to adverse issuer, market, regulatory, or economic developments; foreign investing, especially in emerging markets, has additional risks, such as currency and market volatility and political and social instability; the securities of small companies are subject to higher volatility than those of larger, more established companies; and high-yield bonds are subject to additional risks, such as increased risk of default. Each portfolio's name refers to the approximate retirement year of the investors for whom the portfolio's asset allocation strategy is designed. The portfolios with dates further off initially allocate more aggressively to stock funds. As a portfolio approaches and passes its target date, the allocation will gradually migrate to more conservative, fixed-income funds. The principal value of each portfolio is not guaranteed, and you could lose money at any time, including at, or after, the target date. Liquidity—the extent to which a security may be sold or a derivative position closed without negatively affecting its market value, if at all—may be impaired by reduced trading volume, heightened volatility, rising interest rates, and other market conditions. Hedging and other strategic transactions may increase volatility and result in losses if not successful. Please see the portfolio's prospectus for additional risks.

data.title: Roku: Premium Subscriptions Are Driving Platform Growth
data.description:
2026 has been an especially tough year in the stock market for tech companies. Investors have been debating the impact of AI on enterprise software companies: will customers be able to vibe code custom-built applications instead of paying established software companies to service their needs? But whatever side of this debate you may fall on, we can all agree that AI poses no threat to streaming and entertainment.

Roku (ROKU), the world's #1 streaming TV operating system, demonstrated its resilience in a choppy market in its Q4 earnings print, which featured a slight acceleration in its platform revenue growth as well as a robust profit outlook for FY26. Shares leaped more than 10% after the print, helping to reverse a deep ~30% correction in the stock since December.

I last wrote a buy article on Roku in October, when the stock was trading around $100 per share. Since then, shares of Roku have corrected downward in sympathy with the broader market. But in actuality, Roku's fundamentals have continued at an impressive pace. On the ad side of the business, improving integrations and viewer hours have led to a surge in advertiser demand, while on the content side, the company's additions of a variety of premium subscriptions billed through Roku have helped to boost its monetization. Amid a robust outlook for next year, I remain at a buy rating for Roku: in fact, the recent post-December dip is a great time to buy Roku at a cheaper price, while we're past the volatility of Roku's own earnings cycle.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to Roku, here are what I consider to be the core bull case drivers for the company:

  • Category tailwinds for streaming, while Roku remains platform-agnostic. Despite the prevalence of streaming and Roku's own dominance, streaming hours on the platform continue to grow. Roku will benefit even as streaming platforms continue to compete against each other, as its ecosystem encompasses all channels.
  • Price increases will feed into higher billings for Roku. Netflix (NFLX) has started the trend of raising prices and has done so consistently, prompting other platforms to follow suit. Roku's premium subscriptions business, which bills customers on behalf of Netflix and other platforms, benefits as prices go up.
  • Gross margin upside. Roku's strategy is to pull in viewers through aggressively priced hardware, but to earn profit on these customers over time through their platform revenue. This strategy is working, and we're seeing Roku build up its gross margin.
  • Healthy cash balances. The legacy of Roku's profitability and consistent free cash flow is a ~$2.3 billion cash war chest as of its most recent quarter, worth about 17% of its current market value.

Stay long here: in my view, Roku's upside still has a long road ahead.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Roku's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Roku's revenue grew 16% y/y to $1.39 billion, beating Wall Street expectations of $1.35 billion (+13% y/y) by a three-point margin. Platform revenue, which is what most investors anchor to, grew at an even stronger 18% y/y pace, which accelerated slightly versus 17% y/y growth in Q3.

The core driver behind Roku's platform acceleration is higher attach rates, and broader availability of premium subscriptions - that is, when Roku owners sign up for streaming platforms and have them billed through Roku.

The company has had a number of discrete catalysts here to drive growth. The recent addition of HBO Max to Roku premium subscriptions has brought in additional revenue. Roku is also looking to introduce bundles in 2026, which may encourage upselling while providing consumer offers that aren't available on the individual platforms directly.

Per management's notes in the Q4 shareholder letter:

Q4 marked our biggest quarter ever for Premium Subscriptions net adds, benefiting from holiday promotions and our improvements to the Roku Experience. In October, we expanded Premium Subscriptions to Mexico, initially launching with nine leading SVOD partners. We have since grown the offering to 13 partners, with additional launches planned this year. Looking to 2026, building on the successful addition of HBO Max to Premium Subscriptions, we expect to add more tier-one partners [...]

We also expect to roll out bundles in Premium Subscriptions this year. In Mexico, we anticipate the FIFA World Cup 2026™ to be a driver of Premium Subscriptions, and we will continue expanding into additional international markets."

Roku's continued revenue mix shift away from hardware and into platform has helped to bump gross margins up by 80 bps y/y to 43.5%. At the same time, Roku has also been fierce about cutting expenses: despite double-digit top line growth, Roku cut opex by -2% y/y. As a result, adjusted EBITDA more than doubled y/y to $169.4 million, reflecting a 12.1% margin that improved 560 bps y/y. TTM free cash flow also more than doubled y/y, to $483.6 million (representing more than 100% conversion against adjusted EBITDA of $420.5 million).

Valuation and key takeaways

Despite the optimism contained in Roku's recent trends, I find that the stock still trades at an excellent valuation relative to its growth potential. At post-earnings share prices near $93, Roku trades at a market cap of $13.72 billion. After we net off the $2.32 billion of cash on Roku's latest balance sheet (which is unencumbered of debt), enterprise value goes to just $11.40 billion.

For FY26, Roku has guided to $5.50 billion in revenue and $635 million in adjusted EBITDA, reflecting robust 51% y/y growth in adjusted EBITDA and an 11.5% margin.

If we assume a similar 115% FCF conversion in FY26, FCF would be $730.3 million this year. This positions Roku's valuation multiples at:

  • 18.0x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 15.6x EV/FY26 FCF

To me, these are fantastic multiples for a company that just exited Q4 at a >100% y/y growth rate in adjusted EBITDA. It's clear that Roku is still in the earlier stages of monetization, especially as it prepares to double down on premium subscriptions with new bundle offerings. Stay long here and keep riding the post-earnings optimism higher.

data.title: Updated CBO Outlook Still Flags Deepening Deficits And Rising Fiscal Strain
data.description:
By James Picerno

The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) this week revised its budget outlook for the federal government and the results (still) look worrisome. In line with recent updates, the US deficit remains on track to deteriorate further. The forecast may not be surprising in the red-ink-drenched post-pandemic period, but the new estimates remain concerning by highlighting the government’s deteriorating fiscal profile.

CBO projects that the shortfall will deepen in 2026 to $1.853 trillion, slightly more than 2025’s $1.775 trillion deficit. Looking further down the road offers a similarly concerning trajectory. Over the coming decade, the non-partisan budget office predicts that the US deficit-to-GDP ratio will steepen to 6.7% in 2036 from the current 5.7%.

Policy analysts warn that the size of the deficit is especially troubling because the current level of red ink is unusual for a peacetime economy that’s growing. Presumably during the next recession, if history’s a guide, the fiscal hole will deepen as tax revenue retreats and demand for government spending rises, which is typical for periods when the economy contracts.

The projection for “sustained large deficits are historically unusual, given that the unemployment rate is projected to remain below 5%,” CBO Director Phillip Swagel said in a statement.

A key impact of the growing debt will be reflected in projections for rising interest costs. “Net outlays for interest go from $1.0 trillion in 2026 to $2.1 trillion in 2036, rising from 3.3% of GDP to 4.6%,” Swagel added.

Baseline assumptions

Forecasts are subjective, of course, and so reasonable minds can debate how the future will unfold. CBO’s modeling includes the projection that economic growth will pick up in 2026 (real GDP is expected to rise 2.2% this year, up from 1.9% in 2025), and then downshift to 1.8% for 2027 through 2036. Change the prediction for economic growth and the budget forecast will change, for good or ill, depending.

The White House paints a brighter outlook, claiming that CBO tends to lowball economic growth. “Smaller deficits and higher growth thanks to President Trump’s economic policies are going to slash, not grow, America’s deficit-to-GDP rate,” said White House spokesman Kush Desai.

No matter who’s making the forecast, one constant remains: the future is uncertain. That’s a reminder that the CBO’s forecast can, and probably will, vary from reality, depending on many factors.

What is clear is that the government is running an unusually hefty budget deficit and it’s not obvious that Washington is set to muster the political will to meaningfully trim the red ink. Left unchecked, the deepening shade of red carries non-trivial implications for the years ahead on several fronts, including economic growth, interest rates and inflation.

The Fiscal Risk Index

A crucial task is trying to figure out when (if?) the red ink crosses a red line for financial markets. That’s challenging due to the multiple factors that go into the analytics. Behavioral economics is part of the mix: At what point do investors cry “uncle” and demand higher risk premiums?

For some initial perspective on how a short list of relevant factors stack up, TMC Research monitors the ebb and flow via our Fiscal Risk Index (FRI). The current reading is 0.3 (per the chart below). That’s up from recent history, but still below the previous peak set during the pandemic-related surge of government spending and far below the 1.0 mark, which indicates extreme risk.

Among FRI’s ten inputs (see table below), three are flashing warning signs, relatively speaking, based on our assumptions for modeling the data: dollar weakness, surging gold prices, and a hefty increase in total government debt over the past year.

To be fair, this is one approach to modeling fiscal risk, and it comes with several caveats. First, there are additional inputs that may be relevant to complement the ten used in FRI.

Perhaps more importantly, for any mix of variables and a set of parameters, no one knows where a critical threshold lies that, if crossed, financial markets become dramatically less tolerant of government profligacy.

The main value of FRI is comparing how fiscal risk evolves for a given, if imperfect, measure of fiscal risk vs. history. On that basis, the FRI chart above is useful for comparing how a set of indicators and parameter assumptions have changed and tracking how those conditions shift, or not, in the months and years ahead.

FRI isn’t flashing a crisis today, at least by its own standards, but that could change for the worse, assuming the CBO’s forecast is correct.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: WEX Inc.: Undervalued, But Losing Strategic Ground
data.description:
Investment thesis

It's been almost two years since I last covered WEX (WEX), and while the stock now looks like a clear value bet with depressed multiples and aggressive buybacks, my conviction in the underlying business isn't as strong as it once was. WEX isn't a bad company, but when compared to a similar business and one of its main competitors that appears to be acting more decisively in the most attractive segment of the industry, the relative opportunity becomes hard to ignore, which is why I've assigned it a hold rating.

The Mobility segment overshadowed growth

It's been almost two years since my last coverage of WEX, and during that time the company has gone through a challenging period in terms of growth. But with fiscal year 2025 officially closed, there seems to be light at the end of the tunnel. At first glance, 2025 was a pretty bad year, with sales growing by just over 1%, partly caused by poor Q1 and Q2, where sales decreased over 2% in both quarters, and even during Q1, the gross margin went below the usual 70%, a bad sign and one that had been dragging on since the end of 2024, when sales fell 4%.

Part of the explanation for this performance was that the company lost a major client, which inevitably impacted year-over-year comparisons. However, during Q3 and especially Q4, an improvement in growth was noticeable, with Q3 reporting 4% growth and Q4 reaching 5.7% growth, exceeding analysts' expectations for both sales and profits. This marks what I consider the end of a challenging period for the company.

If we break down the performance by segment, we can see that in Q4 the Mobility segment remained stable but masked the good performance of the Benefits segment, which showed a growth of 10% and Corporate Payments which grew by 18%, while also being the segment with the highest margins in the company. This is because Mobility is tied to fuel prices, which is not ideal considering the long-term trend toward electrification. On the other hand, Corporate Payments benefit from trends such as B2B digitization, cross-border commerce, or accounts payable automation.

This makes sense, as the competitor Fleetcor changed its name to Corpay (CPAY) and has significantly increased its exposure to Corporate Payments in recent years, growing from 28% of total sales in 2022 to nearly 39% by the end of 2025. Meanwhile, WEX has increased its position in the Benefits segment more than other segments, now representing 30% of sales, while Corporate Payments has remained stable at 17-18% of sales. I would like to see this increase, as Corpay appears to be gaining market share (as evidenced by the client lost in that segment this year), and achieving dominance in this market would improve the quality of the company.

So, while Corpay has focused on improving its presence and competitive position through agreements with payment giants like Mastercard and accounts payable automation platforms (you can read about this in my CPAY coverage), WEX has been focused on share buybacks and this year bought 13% of the shares outstanding in addition to the 5% it bought back the previous year. This isn't necessarily a bad thing, as the stock is trading quite cheaply at 5x EV/EBITDA, but perhaps there were other opportunities to use that cash, such as investing in the Corporate Payments segment like Corpay or creating new business lines, especially considering how one of its biggest competitors is moving.

Therefore, it seems like this buyback program could provide significant value to shareholders in the short term, but maybe the company should be moving with a long-term perspective.

Valuation

This year's guidance implies that sales will grow by 3%, and for adjusted EPS, the expected growth would be 9%, which means the company would be trading at a P/E ratio of between 9 and 10 times the adjusted EPS for 2026. But I honestly wouldn't want to take this multiple too seriously because it's full of adjustments, as you can see in the image below, which lists all the adjustments they made to go from GAAP EPS of $8.47 in 2025 to $16.10 (crazy, I know).

So, if we value it by EV/EBIT, because as a payment processor with credit exposure and significant amortization from its acquisitions, free cash flow and net income may be quite volatile and distorted. If we assume modest 4% annual sales growth and EBIT margins remain at the usual 25%, then it would be earning around $800 million in EBIT over five years. With a multiple of 15, below WEX's 5-year average of around 20 and Corpay's average of 17, then the enterprise value would be $12 billion, while the current value is just over $5 billion. This means that the shares could more than double in five years, even with this conservative valuation.

Risks

While the recent stabilization of growth might be encouraging, Mobility still represents roughly half of total revenue, and we've already seen that it's structurally exposed to fuel volumes and pricing dynamics that aren't as attractive as those of Corporate Payments.

It's also worth mentioning the competition with Corpay, which has done a good job repositioning itself in this segment. So, if WEX doesn't accelerate investment in this area, it risks losing its position in what is possibly the fastest-growing segment of the business. This is all without mentioning the competition it faces from other payment processors like Fiserv or indirect competitors like Visa and Mastercard, which aren't purely corporate payments providers but do tend to capture a significant portion of B2B transaction volume.

The bottom line

While WEX could currently be considered a value investment and its multiples are quite depressed, I have to admit I'm not as convinced of the company's quality as I once was. It's not that it's a bad business, but with a perfect comparable company that's better interpreting the situation by identifying the most lucrative and stable segment, I find it difficult to consider WEX a better investment than Corpay, which I've written about previously. That's why I'm leaning towards giving it a hold rating for now. It's not a terrible company by any means so I wouldn't recommend to sell, but I see it as an opportunity cost compared to its biggest competitor.

data.title: AI Bubble, Tech Funeral? Who Will Fail And Who Will Double Down?
data.description:
Sit back and… think about this: an Anthropic (ANTHRO) tech geek adds a plug-in into Claude like "Hey chatbot how are you? Can you now sign documents and review some code?" Things for people wearing thick glasses working late nights in offices ordering take-out pizzas every evening eating at their desks. Not stuff for traders with Red Bull in hand.

What happened in just a couple of days was billions in market capitalization were wiped out from companies whose existence depended upon the sale of Data and Consulting Services as if someone had pressed "delete" on their business model. At once, the realization hit Wall Street that Artificial Intelligence has evolved beyond creating silly kitten videos on skateboards to being a sharp knife cutting through $50,000 per year subscription services and entire departmental structures. The first place it strikes is generally those stocks that for years appeared to be untouchable.

Welcome to Phase II of AI: Fewer Slogans More Natural Selection.

Thesis

Right now, for the companies that provide data consultancy and operate in the field of SaaS (Software as a Service), we are witnessing a major selloff. People may think that it is the AI bubble that bursts, but actually, this is the first genuine stress test for our overvalued service economy. Those who can actually monetize AI will come out stronger, those who have been living off high-fee for and lower incremental value models will be destroyed, and while all of this panic occurs there will be many of the best buy opportunities of the next decade.

A plugin update

Anthropic developed and released Claude Co-Work, an AI Agent System for vertical use cases within the legal and financial industries. The system's features include Contract Analysis, Legal Research, Automatic Memo Generation, Case Triage, etc.

The response to the release of Claude Co-Work from the markets was extremely volatile. Many of the major stocks of the companies that are integral to providing information and data as part of global finance were severely impacted by the loss of market value during a matter of a few trading days ( London Stock Exchange Group (LDNXF) Thomson Reuters (TRI), FactSet (FDS), S&P Global (SPGI), MSCI Inc. (MSCI)).

To better understand how and why investors reacted so quickly to sell-off their shares in these companies, I will first describe the business models of these companies and then explain why they lost so much value so quickly.

For decades, data providers have created a relatively easy-to-understand competitive advantage: they gather extremely high quality data, place it in proprietary systems for access by users, add analytical tools, and then sell it to Wall Street firms at a high price. Professional licenses can cost tens of thousands of dollars per year. I myself pay a much higher annual fee for my Bloomberg terminal than I'm willing to admit. However, I would still be doing so, as the value comes from being able to rely upon the data, having the ability to analyze it in-depth, and ensuring compliance. Those of us who do this type of work, particularly on a professional and institutional basis, such as myself, are required to pay for this type of service.

If a Generative AI system becomes a part of the data ecosystem, an advanced model with both vertical plugins and structured sources can perform much of the operational workload for a firm including creating document summaries, comparing financial reports, completing initial screening processes, and preparing briefing reports. The model will not replace the entire data ecosystem, nor will it replace the decision making process; however, it will complete approximately 80% of the operational workload for firms.

The Era of Chatbots

You can allow your chatbot to complete this work while you are away from your workstation. By the time you return to your workstation, all of the necessary work has been completed. If you have confidence in the chatbot's ability to complete this work, 80% of your workload has been taken care of.

The market, as we have learned, is looking decades into the future and does not care about how the plugin works today but rather how the plugin will function tomorrow. If a plugin costing a few hundred dollars a month can complete approximately 80% of a firm's basic informational needs, the cost difference between this product and a $50,000-$100,000 subscription-based product becomes increasingly difficult to explain. As a result, the price asymmetry between these products decreases, which means there will inevitably be downward pressure on profit margins. Therefore, companies will eventually be faced with a critical decision: either decrease the price of their product and risk losing customers or attempt to differentiate themselves by offering something unique that AI cannot easily replicate (ownership of the data, certification of the data, etc.) to integrate into institutional workflows.

In essence, I believe that the market is not pricing in a revolutionary legal plugin that will change the legal industry but rather the risk of structural compression of recurring revenue streams associated with legal services. This is a classic example of technological disruption, which begins with skepticism regarding the technology, followed by doubt and ultimately a reevaluation of the multiple used to value the technology. As often occurs, the market responds prior to the board of directors responding. The market does not respond based on whether or not the company will prevail, but when a firm's business model appears to become "less defensible," the valuation premium associated with that business model quickly declines.

A service-based economy

As we look to frame the issue, it is critical to understand that the U.S. economy is not a manufacturing economy. Rather, it has evolved into a massive system of services (professional services, finance, law, technology, marketing, etc.) that are now also being exported to foreign markets. As such, AI is a perfect fit for the current state of this service-based economy since it cannot currently perform manual labor tasks; however, AI excels at performing repetitive, automated, informative tasks through data analysis and structurally producing content at times.

The effect of AI in a service-based economy (70-80% of GDP) will be felt throughout the entire model; it will not simply be an influence on a few margins. For this reason, we are seeing large corporations announce mass layoffs of personnel in the technology and professional services sectors over the last six months, while simultaneously announcing their "investment" in AI to increase productivity. Ultimately, this will have a downward pressure on price for services and, at the micro-level, will cause significant challenges to professionals who have grown accustomed to billing at elevated hourly rates and to companies that have become complacent due to organizational inertia.

Consulting firms such as McKinsey & Company, and Accenture (ACN) find themselves in a precarious position. On the one hand, these firms are the primary implementers of the technologies driving the AI revolution for their respective clients; on the other hand, the very business models upon which they rely (billable hour-based) are at risk of being disrupted.

  • McKinsey has already taken steps to begin automating routine and research positions and has reduced its workforce by roughly 10%.
  • Salesforce has followed McKinsey's lead and has eliminated 4,000 support jobs, and replaced those employees with artificial intelligence.

CAPEX Eating Up Cash Flow

I ran a pretty straightforward analysis that someone with Excel can probably do in less than 30 minutes. First, I took the MAG 7 stocks, excluding Tesla, and then I looked at the operating cash flow and CAPEX projections for the upcoming years. Finally, I just divided one figure by the other. For the data, I decided to lean on the Bloomberg terminal only because I think it's very reliable and user-friendly. Actually, for some time now, I've been spending more time on Bloomberg than anywhere else. Anyway, the results are definitely interesting, and I'll try to sum it all up below.

  • In 2024, the companies generated approximately $394 billion of net cash generated from operations and spent approximately $242 billion on CAPEX. We are at 61%, that is high, but also normal for rapidly growing companies.
  • A dramatic shift occurred in 2025 with $437 billion in net cash flow and $394 billion in capital expenditures. At 90%, we're essentially investing almost every dollar that we are generating in cash.
  • In 2026 the projected cash flows are about $409 billion, and the forecasted CAPEX expenditure is approximately $623 billion. That translates into 152% - which means CAPEX spending exceeds the amount of cash flow generated from operations by over 50%.

How do you cover the difference?

In 2026, if they spend $623 billion and produce $409 billion, then there has to be another $200 billion somewhere.

There aren't many choices:

  1. They have enough cash reserves built-up to get by for a while as long as the large tech companies continue to be strong financially.
  2. They can borrow money. Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are all very common names on the bond markets, and I wrote about them in my last article, which I believe was worth reading.
  3. The third choice is either to slow down the buying back of shares (a common practice) or reduce the dividends that are being paid out.

Up until yesterday, the story was, "The big tech companies are paying for the development of AI using the huge amounts of cash that they are generating." These new numbers don't quite tell the same story. I am not saying that it is unsustainable. I am saying that the market is starting to take into account the reality that it is no longer a race that is being funded entirely through profit. Most companies are betting everything on AI. As long as the return that everyone expects comes, the financial health of the company's balance sheet will remain good and the multiples of the stock will increase greatly. However, if the revenues generated by AI are less than what people expect, then the combination of cap ex and debt becomes a credit problem and not simply an equity valuation problem.

The Weak Link

The weak link is clearly Oracle (ORCL) - although I've had no chance to update my review on the stock, which I previously covered here, you will see my position on this as well as all other related positions from my views on the overall economy in several of my economic articles, which detail that I have sold my shares of the stock.

However, for our purposes, the company has essentially become a textbook example of what could be termed a "leveraged AI bet", putting pressure on even its bondholders. As opposed to the ability of companies such as Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta (META) to continue to generate substantial amounts of free cash flow, while they spend on AI; Oracle is the only leading provider of infrastructure with a much more strained profile.

Oracle's debt to equity ratio is at roughly 5x (or 500%), as compared to 0.3 - 0.5x for its stronger peer group.

Its credit default swaps (CDS) have doubled in a matter of months and returned to the highest level they have been at since the 2009 crisis. In terms of the spread of the price of Oracle stocks vs. its bonds, they are priced closer to high-yield junk-bond pricing, rather than safe investment-grade pricing.

Additionally, due to its dependence on a handful of very large AI customers (primarily OpenAI), there is an increased risk of concentration of customer base. The $300 billion deal with OpenAI is at the center of Oracle's growth strategy; however, if OpenAI runs into financial troubles or decides to open up its list of potential partners, then Oracle may be stuck with excess capacity and overwhelming debt commitments.

A leveraged bet on third-party AI essentially.

Selective Massacre

Between 2023 and early 2024, they could just write "AI" in their presentation and get right behind the other players in the major thematic trade. Goldman Sachs showed that stock prices related to AI were correlated at around an 80% level; therefore, buying any one of the "correctly positioned" names was essentially buying the whole basket.

That correlation has dropped to approximately 20% today. The market has ceased treating AI as a single ETF and has started to clearly differentiate between those that:

  • Can monetize their AI, immediately increasing both their revenue and profit margins, and
  • Are burning large amounts of cash for massive, AI-based projects with no immediate, or obvious, path to profitability.

Meta Platforms' and Microsoft's recent quarterly earnings have provided a good example of the distinction between investing in AI, and monetizing AI. Both increased capital expenditures, but the market reaction to each company's actions has been the exact opposite. Meta's use of AI has increased the efficiency of its ad platform which has driven an increase in Meta's revenues. Microsoft and Alphabet's costs of building and using an artificial intelligence (AI) system are increasing at a rate greater than their traditional revenue streams. Microsoft has stated it is generating positive free cash flow, but the market is penalizing the company because there is no clear consensus on whether the Copilot service or the Azure AI infrastructure will generate a large amount of net income anytime soon. In some cases companies develop the capability prior to the time the market can absorb it and therefore develop margin compression.

The old story first mantra has now been replaced by a blunt show me the money.

SaaSpocalypse

The SaaS area is being hit hardest and it has dropped 28 percent from its highs, a decline that hasn't been seen since the 2008 financial crisis. This is the result of how AI could eat into the business models of Salesforce (CRM), ServiceNow (NOW), DocuSign (DOCU), etc., as all three license by seat. Because if AI can make employees more efficient, then client companies will require fewer software seats for the same amount of work, and this will negatively impact the revenue of SaaS providers.

In addition, profits haven't completely disappeared, as Salesforce is still growing at double digit rates, ServiceNow is producing over 20% revenue growth and has near perfect customer retention, and Adobe is integrating generative models throughout its suite. However, what has changed is the baseline expectations of investors regarding the market. With AI offering potential to make employees more efficient, clients may need fewer software seats to accomplish the same amount of work.

As long as the market is thinking about this, the best course of action is to sell the SaaS stocks now -- before the multiples lose the parachute of "TINA + AI + infinite growth" at zero interest rate.

Logistics

The logistics industry is last on this list, however, the AI Scare Trade has also had a negative impact on it. We have always believed that the logistics industry was "AI-Resistant", given the physical nature of the work; however, after Algorhythm Holdings, Inc. (RIME) announced via press release their SemiCab platform, the entire logistics industry rapidly collapsed.

Based upon collected data, AI integration has enabled a 300% to 400% increase in cargo volume with no increase in the number of employees operating within the logistics operations. The use of AI technology enables a single freight broker to manage approximately 2,000 loads in a calendar year (the industry standard is 500 loads in a calendar year).

You also know that freight brokers generally act as an intermediary between the shipper (or receiver) and the carrier (or motor carrier), facilitating the connection between these two parties; coordinating load movement and negotiations among all involved parties. Freight brokers derive their value from organizing and managing the process. If an AI platform could perform the same function as a freight broker - matching supply and demand, routing optimization, reduction in down time - then there would be less need for a middleman to facilitate these activities.

The fate of the markets

The last question being asked by the markets is not "if" AI will burst, but "when" the bubble will burst and who will it affect.

I want to tell you something: I do not have the slightest idea of when the bubble will pop and if it will pop. I do not know who will be damaged or if there will be any casualties from the market crash. Truthfully, no one can ever tell you that. However, we should expect to see sensationalized articles and videos popping up all over the web about the "End of Tech", "End of AI", etc., possibly as early as tomorrow due to the high levels of anxiety and uncertainty the market is experiencing right now.

I am 100% confident that technology will win out. It will be the companies that cannot develop a viable business model for their products/services that will go bankrupt. In the late 90s/early 2000s, we saw numerous companies fail because of unsustainable business models. Companies such as Amazon and Google were able to survive and become the dominant players in today's economy.

We are currently in the second or third year of the AI cycle, and the market is now in a state of morbid fear. Having a cash cushion is now a necessity to take advantage of the opportunity presented to us in the market. Those that sell their stocks at reduced prices during this time of panic will be some of the best buying opportunities of the decade, provided we can identify them using their balance sheet and proprietary data. Diversification is still necessary, however, it is not required to diversify using generalist indexes that include both industry leaders and tech "zombies".

The point is that the problems with the service economy existed for years prior to anyone realizing the difficulties; thus artificial intelligence did not create the problem, but merely pointed out the problems.

Conclusion

As an investor, I began taking action as early as the end of 2025, because in my 2026 analysis, I stated that 2026 would be the year of choice, and that within the same sectors, some companies will do very well and other companies will do poorly.

I then broke down the balance sheet, cash flow, leverage, and overall business quality of each company to determine which companies have the ability to withstand the coming economic turmoil and which do not. I also recognize that retail investors may be not able to analyze companies as institutional investors, so......here is my checklist, which represents my daily routine of examining financial statements and monitoring stock price movements:

  • Free Cash Flow vs. AI Capital Expenditures
  • Leverage, Debt Level & Timing
  • Is AI being monetized?
  • Pricing Model and Customer Retention
  • Quality of the "ex-AI" Core Business

One important fact to keep in mind is that fear and opportunity, are the same room. The only difference is the seat at the table that you occupy when the next "SaaSpocalypse" is a trending topic. Are you going to be sitting at the table pressing your sell button, or are you going to be sitting at the table with balance sheets and credit spreads, ready to buy?

Good luck with your investing journey.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Inflation Preview: Get Ready For CPI
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The trading world awaits the final piece to this week's US economic puzzle, with the US Consumer Price Index (CPI) releasing tomorrow at 8:30 A.M. (ET). After a cold retail sales (0% vs. 0.4% exp) and a surprisingly strong non-farm payrolls report (130K vs. 70K exp), the third wave of high-tier US data should provide the answers for early 2026 rate cut pricing (and if they are even still a thing).

The Federal Reserve had pivoted within its dual mandate, putting less emphasis on the inflation mandate and prioritizing a visibly weakening labor market, to justify the streak of 25 bps cuts between September and December 2025, from 4.50% to the current 3.75%.

Ahead of these cuts, the Fed held a frustrating pause since December 2024. After the massive -140K drop in October, non-farm payrolls rebounded through a gradual recovery and haven't reported a figure below +40K since. Hence, communications from the Fed quickly turned back to inflation.

Core CPI, which excludes volatile energy and food prices from the headline index, remained much closer to 3% than the 2% mandate throughout 2025, with the effects of the infamous Trump tariffs delaying post-COVID inflation drops further (compared to what was seen in Canada, for example). And that hasn't sat well with the Fed.

Expectations for tomorrow's report

Tomorrow's report should bring further clarity to the US CPI releases, which had previously been obscured by the longest-ever Government shutdown in October, which prevented precise data collection.

Both the headline and core CPI are expected to release at +0.3% m/m, taking the year-over-year data to 2.5% for both. Comforting for the Fed? Maybe, but this release should also be affected by last year's high base effect (January 2025 core CPI was at 3.1%!), and hence provide more sensitivity to any beat.

Producer prices tend to be reflected in CPI between 3 and 6 months after their rise, but the more lasting effects may even take more than a year. For those who forgot, PPI sent out a major warning about tariffs in July 2025: We are entering the final periods to observe significant effects from the Trump policies, and the Fed will be watching closely.

It has a few of the most hawkish regional members on the voting chairs in 2026 (Hammack and Logan), so thresholds for cuts will be high.

Markets are at a sensitive inflection point, with equities still at or very close to their all-time highs (despite huge routs in the tech and software sectors) and extreme trends looking fragile, as seen this morning with the large drops in gold and silver.

If things turn sour, stock and metal markets could quickly see a crypto-like bear market for the foreseeable future, as participants will be forced to deleverage. Let's discover reactions to see why.

Reaction scenarios

The following FOMC rate decision will be announced on March 18 and is currently priced at only 8% (from 23% pre-NFP), leaving little room for a correction at this meeting. However, there are still about two cuts fully priced in for the rest of the year.

Markets are impossible to predict fully; hence, these scenarios could elicit quite different reactions.

As expected

This would kick the can down the road for policy pricing, being the final influential data release before February 27 (PPI).

Stock Indexes:

An as-expected data will send stock markets to a quick test of their recent all-time highs, but the effect should slow down throughout the session. After the data, expect indexes to keep consolidating at their highs (48,000 to 50,000 for the DJIA) while waiting for more clarity.

Metals:

After today's large drop, metals should continue seeing progressive outflows - silver back to $70 and gold hanging around $4,200 to $4,400

Cryptos:

Cryptos should rally initially, but the rise shouldn't be long-lasting. It could at least prevent a consistent drop lower, back to a more range-bound action in Bitcoin (between $70,000 and $80,000).

US dollar:

Initial push lower but should stabilize above 2026 lows before reverting higher and maintaining its range-bound trajectory.

Rate cut pricings:

The March meeting should see its pricing rise back towards 20%. Further changes will be contingent on a larger rise depending on the state of markets until future data lands (if stocks dip strongly, expect rate cut odds to rise)

Inflation beats

This is the worst-case scenario for all assets that have rallied throughout the 2025 debasement trade. Stocks, metals and cryptos should all drop lower, with short-end yields rising strongly in a bear flattening curve, profiting the US dollar (rallying back to at least 98.00).

The extent of the moves will depend on how strong the beat is. +0.1% to expectations should see modest moves, but anything above 0.3% will see large repricings. Expect to only see one more cut priced in for 2026 in that scenario.

Inflation misses

This is what markets are salivating for the most. Stock indexes should all rally to some new all-time highs, with the Dow Jones potentially testing 51,000. Nasdaq should also retest its 26,200 and even extend further.

The US dollar will see a shift drop to some new 2026 lows as pricing for a March meeting cut can rise to above 50%. Cryptos and metals should also see a decent rebound in that event, profiting from the drop in the US dollar.

Both asset classes would, however, need a significant drop in CPI to head back to their all-time highs, with both of their markets troubled by pessimistic sentiment and positioning.

After swift rises, the action should settle, with leverage and positioning still elevated. Nevertheless, expect the recent descending trends to cease and sentiment to ease. The extent of such reactions, of course, depends on how large the miss is.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Asbury Automotive's Resilience Is Undervalued
data.description:
Shares of Asbury Automotive Group (ABG) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 23% of their value. While the company has delivered solid financial results, its focus on M&A over share repurchases has not been well received by investors. I have argued that concerns about its M&A strategy are overdone and left valuation compelling, which is why I rated shares a “strong buy” in September. This thesis has not played out, with the stock dropping a further 10% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit ABG to determine if performance can improve or if investors should cut losses. I remain bullish given the valuation.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Asbury Automotive earned $6.67, which was a penny ahead of expectations as revenue grew 4% to $4.7 billion. For the full year, the company earned $28.10, whereas I was targeting $27-28. While the stock has underperformed, financial performance has been in-line. Gross profit grew 6% to a record $793 million even as same-store revenue declined 6% due to weaker new vehicle sales. It continues to roll-out its Tekion technology system, which is now at more than 25% of locations, which is helping to drive incremental margin gains. This roll-out will temporarily increase H1 spending and then reduce H2 spending as dealerships switch over and enjoy efficiency benefits. As a reminder, ABG operates 171 dealerships primarily in the Sun Belt, though it has also grown its exposure to other markets, most notably Massachusetts.

While new car sales are highly cyclical, the auto dealership model is far more resilient than widely appreciated, in my view. As you can see below, new and used car sales account for about 80% of revenue. Frankly, this revenue, particularly on the new car side, may face some pressure in 2026. While I do not expect a recession, affordability challenges continue, especially for lower- and middle-income consumers. While high in revenue, car sales are relatively low margin. They only account for 28% of gross profit. Parts & service work is half of the profit center, and activity here is much less economically sensitive. Because of this, Asbury’s profits and cash flows are much less cyclical than one would think given the perception around car sales. In some respects, dealers sell cars to start a long-running service relationship with customers.

Looking into segment data, new car revenues fell 6% on a same-store basis, and gross profit was down 20% as margins per car continue to normalize from post-COVID highs as supply pressures have fully eased. Inventory is up slightly at a 52-day supply vs. 49 last year, but this is still a healthy level—I get concerned if inventory is outside of a 45-60 day range. ABG’s business is increasingly skewed towards the high-end, with luxury brands making up 36% of sales vs. 32% last year. With higher-income consumers having more resilience to ongoing affordability challenges, I view this upscale trend as positive and a reason why we are seeing gross margins improve. Management expects a slightly lower pace of new car sales this year, consistent with my view.

Used same-store retail gross profit grew 4% to $51 million even as revenue declined 10%. That is because same-store retail gross profit per unit was up 18% to $1,749. Used selling prices were up a strong 6% from last year to $33k, though this largely reflected a mix shift. I have expected to see some margin expansion here as affordability challenges and tariffs push some consumers down into the used car market. That incremental demand should provide some pricing power, which we appear to be seeing, helping to partially mitigate some of the normalization pressures on the new car side.

Parts & Service same-store sales rose 2% with a 3% gross profit gain, given modest ongoing margin expansion. Still, the pace of growth here was a bit disappointing, especially considering how critical of a profit center this unit is. Looking at sub-segment data, collision activity was especially weak, and this has also coincided with auto insurers reporting strong results. Collision activity is somewhat random quarter to quarter, but over time, accident frequency and severity should average out.

Warranty activity remains particularly strong, and this is probably the least economically sensitive portion of the business since the manufacturers have to foot the bill. Recalls and more warranty activity are a positive for dealerships as a result of this. While still positive, customer pay work was a bit weaker than I like to see. To some extent, consumers may be deferring maintenance activity (i.e., replacing tires or changing oil a bit more slowly) as they watch budgets. On the earnings call, management noted that activity accelerated in January, and a strong tax refund season may lead to some pent-up demand being filled. I expect this to run closer to 5% in 2026, but it bears watching.

During 2025, Asbury generated $465 million of free cash flow, which helped fund its Herb Chalmers M&A transaction. Its balance sheet is in a healthy position, thanks to some divestitures, with leverage at 3.2x, though this is a bit above its 2.5-3.0x target. There are another 9 locations it expects to divest in Q1, and total divestitures will be $750 million. The company aims to get leverage down below 3x by the end of 2026, which I expect to occur. During the fourth quarter, ABG repurchased $50 million of stock, and barring significant M&A, I expect repurchases to continue at a similar or faster pace this year. I would expect the company to use divestiture proceeds to pay down some of its 2026 debt while refinancing the rest.

In December, Asbury announced it was promoting its COO to be its next CEO, and the outgoing CEO, David Hult, will transition to being executive Chair for a period of time. This is an orderly process, consistent with a well-thought out succession plan, and with ABG promoting an insider and retaining its former CEO as Chair, I do not expect a material shift if the company’s strategic focus as a result of this change.

Back in September, I estimated ABG had ~$24 of long-term EPS, once vehicle margins fully returned to normal levels. With faster debt reduction in H1 and Tekion benefits beginning to take hold, I am looking for $26.50-$28.50 of EPS this year, assuming ~5% parts & services growth. I now view run-rate EPS as being closer to $25, aided by share count reduction. While moderating, the new car selling environment is still likely to be better than normal in 2026, aiding margins and revenue to a degree.

Shares are just 8.5x 2026 EPS and ~9.4x normalized EPS. For a business that gets the majority of its profitability from less economically sensitive sectors, I view this as a very attractive valuation. I continue to view this as an attractive level. Plus with leverage coming inside of the target range, there is ample capacity for ABG to buy back stock after completing in-process divestitures to capitalize on this discount. Ay a ~10% free cash flow yield, ABG is a compelling value for long-term investors, and as investors gradually appreciate the resilience of its cash flow, I expect shares to move towards $300 or 12x run-rate earnings, meriting a strong buy rating.

data.title: Allspring Growth Fund Q4 2025 Fund Performers And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Allspring Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Top Contributors and Detractors to Quarter-end Fund Performance

CONTRIBUTORS

  • Carpenter Technology Corp.
  • Natera, Inc.
  • BridgeBio Pharma, Inc. (BBIO)
  • argenx SE
  • Penumbra, Inc. (PEN)

DETRACTORS

  • Alphabet Inc.
  • Varonis Systems, Inc. (VRNS)
  • Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN)
  • Oracle Corp.
  • DoorDash, Inc.
The holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased or sold during the period shown and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. Information on calculation methodology and a list showing the overall contribution of each holding in the account for the period shown are available upon request.

Discussion of Contributors

Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS) is prepared for lift-off. The company manufactures premium metal alloys used in highly specialized components across aerospace, medical, and industrial markets. While this diversification provides a degree of demand stability, our investment thesis focuses on the significant replacement cycle in aerospace—a sector that has faced years of underinvestment and production delays. The post-pandemic surge in air travel has led airlines to keep aging fleets in service longer, driving higher maintenance needs. This has boosted demand for Carpenter’s suite of specialty hardware, including bearings, fasteners, and electrical components. Producing these advanced alloys is both complex and capital-intensive, giving Carpenter meaningful pricing power—a fact underscored this quarter as multiple long-term contracts were repriced favorably. With robust product demand and expanding margins, we remain confident in Carpenter’s long-term growth prospects.

Natera, Inc. (NTRA), is redefining precision in diagnostics. A molecular diagnostics company, Natera specializes in three main areas of health care: women’s health, oncology, and organ health. The women’s health division commands nearly half of the noninvasive prenatal testing market, generating wide margins that have fueled innovation across other segments. Leveraging in-house expertise, Natera developed Signatera, a liquid biopsy that detects minimal residual disease and can identify cancer recurrence months before traditional methods. The blood-based test currently holds FDA and Medicare approvals for less than a dozen types of cancer, yet adoption surged during the year due to the minimally invasive approach and early detection capabilities. With additional approvals pending, Natera doubled the number of oncology sales representatives to drive awareness and volumes in 2026. Backed by cutting-edge diagnostics and a vast, underpenetrated market, we believe that Natera is well positioned to transform cancer care and deliver sustained growth for investors.

Immunology and returns are boosting with argenx SE (ARGX). As a commercial-stage biopharmaceutical company, argenx is focused on developing therapies for chronic immunological conditions. Its flagship treatment, VYVGART, targets the harmful antibodies causing disease without shutting down the entire immune system—giving patients strong results with fewer side effects compared with other treatments. Initially, VYVGART was administered intravenously at ambulatory or infusion centers but recently became available in prefilled dosages (PFS). Far from cannibalizing existing IV usage, VYVGART PFS has expanded the company’s addressable market by improving access for patients in communities without local infusion centers. As VYVGART gains traction with new patients, argenx continues to advance its pipeline, targeting additional autoimmune disorders to further broaden its impact.

Discussion of Detractors

AI is spelling opportunity for Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL). In early 2025, investor sentiment declared that Google search was all but dead, replaced by large language models (LLMs) and AI models. Yet, with decades of search data on file, Alphabet had the key components and talent to produce its own top-notch LLM. In September, Google released the “Gemini Drop,” which included new features such as Nano Banana image editing. Daily downloads of Gemini usurped those of ChatGPT, capturing a sliver of the market. Sentiment for Alphabet was further buoyed after the Department of Justice found that Google monopolized search advertising markets yet allowed the company to maintain lucrative partnerships with Chrome or Android. While the quarter was positive for Alphabet, our relative underweight meaningfully detracted from returns. We added to the position during the quarter but have maintained a relative underweight due to the rapidly shifting AI landscape.

Oracle Corp. (ORCL) cools on circular concerns. After years of steady but modest growth from legacy systems, Oracle reinvented itself by transforming its global data center network into Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. In early 2025, the company joined forces with AI heavyweights—including NVIDIA (NVDA) and OpenAI—through the Stargate Project to build next-generation AI-focused data centers across the U.S. Momentum continued as Oracle expanded strategic partnerships with Microsoft (MSFT) and Bloom Energy. This peaked in September when Oracle announced a five-year, $300 billion contract with OpenAI, marking one of the largest AI infrastructure deals ever and the first to be funded primarily through debt. Investor response was initially enthusiastic—the stock spiked over 40%—but the rally reversed as concerns emerged over Oracle’s capital intensity and circular dependency risk. Reflecting these dynamics, we reduced our position due to increased risk and volatility while maintaining conviction that Oracle’s stable core business offers steady cash flow and its AI endeavors provide optionality and growth potential. With improving profitability and clarity on large deals, we believe Oracle can reestablish itself as an AI front-runner in the long term.

DoorDash, Inc. (DASH), hit a speed bump on its growth journey.

The company continues to leverage its scale and advanced logistics platform to expand beyond restaurant delivery into convenience, grocery, and retail categories. While core order volumes accelerated and top-line trends remained strong, the recent quarter was overshadowed by elevated spending on international expansion, technology upgrades, and new verticals. These investments pressured margins and tempered near-term profitability, sparking investor concerns despite healthy demand fundamentals. Longer term, we believe DoorDash’s diversification strategy—coupled with global market penetration and leadership in last-mile logistics—positions it to capture significant share in the evolving delivery ecosystem.

The views expressed and any forward-looking statements are as of December 31, 2025, and are those of the fund managers and/or Allspring Global Investments. Discussions of individual securities or the markets generally are not intended as individual recommendations. Future events or results may vary significantly from those expressed in any forward-looking statements. The views expressed are subject to change at any time in response to changing circumstances in the market. Allspring Global Investments disclaims any obligation to publicly update or revise any views expressed or any forward-looking statements.

Carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses before investing. For a current prospectus and, if available, a summary prospectus, containing this and other information, visit Allspring Global Investments: Investment Management & Services . Read it carefully before investing.

Allspring Global Investments™ is the trade name for the asset management firms of Allspring Global Investments Holdings, LLC, a holding company indirectly owned by certain private funds of GTCR LLC and Revere Capital Partners, L.P. These firms include but are not limited to Allspring Global Investments, LLC, and Allspring Funds Management, LLC. Certain products managed by Allspring entities are distributed by Allspring Funds Distributor, LLC (a broker-dealer and Member FINRA/SIPC).
